entry.title: Re: Profit Center Transfer via Stock Transfer Order
entry.content:
Hello,

It's standard sap function. For standard stock transfer 2 steps, the accounting document is on 641 movement type. While 101 only move for qty only. Without doing this you can't see stock in transit in MB52 or MB5T.

entry.title: Re: Enabling your workforce for the future with the new integrated learning experience
entry.content:
Hi,

we have currently a mismatch of programs in the ILX. The case: When an employee registers to a duration-based program with Required Type "REQ", we see different outcomes in the database and reporting on the one hand and its display on the new learning home page. In our case we can't change the required type, as we also use this program in APs. 

Display in ILX: This program shows up in the "invest in myself" bucket, because it is self assigned.

Reporting: In the reports learning plan and learning needs, as well as in the admin view, the program is defined as required type "REQ". For manager reports this is highly misleading, as for managers it might look like, that the program is required to finish - which is cleary not always the case, as it was self assigned.

Is there any way to align this mismatch? Ideally that self assigned programs, do not inherit the "REQ"-Required type of the program, when self assigned.

"0001236012"|"lusy"|"lams"|"dummy2@gmail.com"|"3847667584"

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<Employees>

      <EmployeeID>000123678</EmployeeID>

entry.title: Fiori Elements Analytical table: Column label text VS Colum label text when grouped
entry.content:
Hi gurus,

I have a Fiori Elements App which uses an Analytical table (With an underlying a CDS + Cube + Query). By using annotation @ui.presentationVariant I am able to having a default grouping, which is good, but am experiencing an undesired effect regarding column labels.

There is one field, for which I specified a custom label 'Billing Document' in the Query to overwrite the labels that inherit from the SAP data element.

Also, this field is one of the used for the the default grouping, as follows:

My issue is that when the app runs and the grouping is applied, the label shown in the column (The custom one set in the Query) does not match with the one shown in the Group (The standard one as per the data element) so it is a bit confusing.

Here I'm highlighting in green the other columns used for grouping and where the labels match, and in yellow this case in which the custom label is not being set in the grouping, only in the column.

Does somebody know If I'm missing some additional annotation? I was not able to figure it out yet.

Thanks a lot!

entry.title: Re: How to Retrieve Key Performance Metrics from SAP ASE...
entry.content:
The version of the Python interpreter I was running was 3.9

Re: Can't run sp_monitorconfig from within a transaction

I think that means you have autocommit set to True (automatically run "begin transaction" before each SQL statement, run "commit transaction" after each SQL statement).   As the error message says, that doesn't work with sp_monitorconfig.  Try running it with autocommit set to False (connect with autocommit set to False):

https://stackoverflow.com/questions/33713800/pyodbc-autocommit-does-not-appear-to-work-with-sybase-and-sqlalchemy

entry.title: Re: Concerns regarding GRID SBP upgrade from 19.16 to 19.20
entry.content:
Dear Customer,

Please find below the answers:

1. Please take full database backup with BR*tools.

2. Yes.

3. Filesystem backup is not enough, please take full backup with BR*tools.

To revert to a previous, older SAP Bundle Patch, follow the installation instructions of that older SAP Bundle Patch.

Best regards,
SAP Support

entry.title: Re: "WA_TOP1" cannot be converted to the row type of "IT_...
entry.content:
You declared 

it_top1 TYPE TABLE OF slis_t_listheader,

So it_top1 is a table of tables of records of structure slis_listheader (slis_t_listheader is already a table type definition)

entry.title: Re: FKKBIX: error FKKBIX2537 during the creation of a BIT...
entry.content: Check your settings in customizing table VC_TFK8260. The cost type OBC is not maintained, but BITTYPE OBC is used for the BIT2. The cost type and the BITTYPE must be the same. Just read F1 help of the field 'Type of Billable Item' in VC_TFK8260.

entry.title: How to Test Your Solution for Breakage
entry.content:
Browsers Are Ending Third-Party Cookie Support

As you might be aware already, browser manufacturers are ending support of third-party cookies. Google Chrome is the most prominent example, with its third-party cookie deprecation program planned for the end of 2024. This might impact your SAP solutions especially regarding session management and single sign-on authentication, as we mentioned in the first installment of our blog series. If you haven’t already, we recommend reading through it to know whether your solution is affected by the third-party cookie deprecation.

In our second installment, we’ll take you through the timeline communicated by Google, and provide you with recommendations to prepare, test for breakage, and minimize the impact on your SAP solution.

 

What is the Timeline?

Here is a timeline with the most important dates you should have in mind:

  • July 2024: End of the grace period.
  • January 2025: Third-party cookie deprecation ramp-up to 100% of all users.

Why Testing Is Important?

Cookies are used for various purposes in many applications. From session information to technical authentication flows, there are many ways an application might be affected from the deprecation of third-party cookies. Thus, we highly recommend you thoroughly test the behavior of your application.

SAP recommends following a three-steps approach:

  1. Test for breakage after the end of the grace period, starting July 1st 2024.
  2. Test for breakage starting January 2025, when third-party cookie deprecation is fully rolled out.
  3. If needed, audit your solution for third-party cookies.

Because SAP has secured deprecation trial tokens for the major domains used by SAP BTP, it is likely that your application will run fine until the end of the deprecation trial period. We recommend making sure that your application continues to run smoothly, even after the temporary measures run out by testing with specific configurations.

These testing steps should give you an idea of how your solution could be affected by the third-party cookie deprecation. We strongly recommend getting a deprecation trial token if your solution is affected, as it will not only allow you to keep things running smoothly until the end of the grace period, but also to prepare you for proper testing before the third-party cookie deprecation is fully rolled out by January 2025. Additionally, this will buy you some time to think of a solution recommended by Google, like CHIPS or Storage Access API (SAA) before the end of the grace period.

We have come up with a proper testing guide that should help you get started with testing. For more information and the full detailed procedure, see Preparing and Testing Your Solution for Third-Party Cookie Deprecation on the help portal.

 

What Should You Do After Testing?

If your notice a breakage after testing, consider Google’s deprecation trial period, during which you can opt-out of the third-party cookie deprecation, request for additional time to migrate away from third-party cookie dependencies, investigate on potential solutions for your application and implement a final solution. If eligible, Google will provide you with a deprecation trial token. We will provide more info on the deprecation trial tokens in an upcoming blog.

This blog is the second part of our blog series covering the third-party cookie deprecation:

  • Are You Affected by the Third-Party Cookie Deprecation? 
  • How to test your solution for breakage. Full testing guide is now available: Preparing and Testing Your Solution for Third-Party Cookie Deprecation .
  • How to handle deprecation trial tokens. (Coming Soon)
  • How to prepare your application for the blockage of third-party cookies. (Coming Soon)

Keep watching this space for more information regarding Google’s third-party cookie deprecation.

entry.title: Re: SQL Queries with Service Layer returns error code 402
entry.content:
We had the same problem after updating from fp2110 to sp2402. We did this (from troubleshooting note 3064927 - this part is same both on linux and windows, different folders):

    • The issue could be related to corrupted SQLQueries config file. Goto ServiceLayer Installation folder (%ProgramFiles%\SAP\SAP Business One ServerTools\ServiceLayer\Conf\).
    • Rename the file b1s_sqltable.conf to b1s_sqltable_backup.conf
    • Open b1s_sqltable.template.conf and save as b1s_sqltable.conf.
    • Restart the Service Layer Service.

After that b1s/v1/SQLQueries worked well, but we had to manually edit the b1s_sqltable.conf file and add some tables that we needed for our queries.

entry.title: Multi-tier Collaborative Outsourcing Manufacturing Process with SAP Business Network
entry.content:
Why Outsourcing Manufacturing Process

Outsourcing manufacturing processes offers several benefits that can enhance a company's operational efficiency, cost-effectiveness, and overall competitiveness.

  1. Cost Savings:
  • Lower Labour Costs: Outsourcing to countries with lower labour costs can significantly reduce production expenses.
  • Reduced Overhead: Outsourcing eliminates the need for investment in expensive machinery, factory space, and other infrastructure.
  1. Focus on Core Competencies:
  • Companies can concentrate on their core business activities, such as research and development, marketing, and sales, while leaving the manufacturing to specialized firms.
  1. Access to Expertise and Technology:
  • Outsourcing partners often have specialized skills, advanced technologies, and industry expertise that can improve product quality and innovation.
  1. Scalability and Flexibility:
  • Companies can quickly scale production up or down in response to market demand without the constraints of fixed costs associated with owning and operating manufacturing facilities.
  1. Risk Mitigation:
  • Outsourcing can spread risk by diversifying the supply chain, reducing the impact of localized disruptions.
  • It also reduces the financial risk of investing in manufacturing facilities and technology that may become obsolete.
  1. Faster Time to Market:
  • Specialized manufacturers can often produce goods more quickly due to their expertise and streamlined processes, speeding up time-to-market for new products.
  1. Access to Global Markets:
  • Outsourcing can provide access to international markets, enabling companies to establish a global presence and reach customers more effectively.
  1. Regulatory Compliance:
  • Outsourcing partners, particularly those with experience in international markets, are often well-versed in regulatory requirements, helping companies comply with local laws and standards.
  1. Capital Conservation:
  • By avoiding the large capital expenditures associated with building and maintaining manufacturing facilities, companies can conserve capital for other strategic investments.
  1. Enhanced Supply Chain Management:
  • Outsourcing can lead to more efficient supply chain management by leveraging the expertise of specialized firms in logistics and supply chain optimization.

Multi-tier Collaborative Outsourcing Manufacturing Process with SAP Business Network

Multi-tier Collaborative Outsourcing Manufacturing on SAP Business Network involves coordinated efforts across multiple tiers of suppliers and manufacturers, enhancing visibility, communication, and efficiency throughout the supply chain. This approach leverages the integration of SAP Business Network with SAP S/4HANA to facilitate seamless collaboration and optimize the entire production process.

  • Provide CMO’s with near real time visibility into demand forecast.
  • Enable CMO’s to commit to buyer’s requested forecast quantities to manage constrained planning process.
  • Visibility for CMOs WIP (Work-in-progress) Inventory data, Component inventory visibility ,  drop shipment process between Component Supplier and CMO etc.
  • Management of PO Batch # Assignment for batch genealogy, Earlier visibility into batch quality and results
  • Enables contract manufacturers to report receipt and consumption of components with relevant batch information or Serial no information as relevant.

Processes Steps

Planning Process

  • The Planner of the Buying Organization perform the planning for FG to be produced according to the planning cycle/ horizon. During the planning process, considering the In-house plant capacity and available resources planner take decision on In-house production as well as forecast the allocated Quantities to be manufactured by the by the CMO (Contract Manufacturers) in SAP IBP.
  • The Contract Manufactures can provide the commitment to the Forecast depending on available capacity. They can also provide upside/downside capacity availability on SAP Business Network.
  • The Planner of the buying organization can re-plan and adjust the allocation as per CMOs forecast commitments and capacity availabilities in SAP IBP.

Multi-tier Purchase Order for Components Process

  • The BOM (Bill of Material) explosions for the forecast committed Contract Manufacturing plan / quantity of FG (Finished Goods) is performed and the Quantity of each components required is derived. Based on availability of Inventory of the components, the multi-tier purchase order for components is released to the component suppliers and copy is shared with CMO for transparency and visibility.
  • The Component supplier would be directly shipping the components to the CMO location / plant as mentioned in the multi-tier purchase order.
  • The CMO would receive the components against the Multi-tier Component Purchase order on Business Network and corresponding receiving document would be created automatically in Buyer’s SAP S4HANA by giving visibility component inventory present at the CMO premise.

Copy Supplier – Multi-tier Purchase Order in SAP S4HANA

Drop Ship the Components to Directly to CMO

Multi-tier PO Visibility to CMO as well as Components supplier on SAP Business Network

Components Receipt by CMO referencing to ASN created by Componenet Supplier

Component Inventory at CMO Premise on SAP Business Network

Sub-Contracting Purchase Order for FG production to CMO.

  • The buyer would create a Sub-Contract Purchase Order (Item Type L) for manufacturing of FG (Finished Good) product to the CMO. The Sub-contract order would have BOM details / components required for production / assembly of FG product.
  • The CMO would start production process steps / assembly or fabrication process steps. CMO can share the WIP – Work-in-progress Inventory details using Manufacturing visibility functionality of SAP Business Network
  • The CMO can report the Realtime Component consumption details during the FG production processes. The SAP business network support both real time ( WIP level) consumption posting as well as Backflush scenario ( all Component consumption posting at the end of FG production).
  • This process provides component consumption tracking , Component inventory & WIP inventory visibility and reconciliation of Components etc.
  • During this process, batch genealogy or Serial number tracking are maintained as necessary. For perishable items, the self-life can be tracked too.
  • The Delivery of FG with ASN process as well as digital invoice submission for CMO is supported on SAP Business Network.

Sub-contracting PO on SAP Business Network

Component Consumption Reporting by CMO

Integrations between S4HANA and SAP Business Network for Sub-Contracting Process

Integrating SAP S/4HANA with SAP Business Network for the subcontracting process using the Cloud Integration Gateway (CIG) involves below steps.

References :

Explaining Subcontracting Collaboration

Demonstrating the Subcontracting with Multi-Tier Collaboration Process

Component Planning Collaboration with SAP Business Network

entry.title: Explore and Infuse DOX capabilities in SAP BTP custom applications
entry.content:
Introduction to Document Information Extraction

Document Information Extraction (a.k.a DOX) comes to rescue when we need to process large amounts of business documents that have content in headers, line items and tables. Extracted information can be further used, for example, to automatically process payables, invoices, or payment notes while making sure that invoices and payables match. After uploading a document file to the service, it returns the extraction results from header fields and line items. Leverage the power of Generative AI to automate the information extraction with the DOX Premium edition.

Plan offerings for DOX 

Base plan: Base Edition service plan includes all core features but doesn't include document information extraction using generative AI. 

  • Service plan intended for productive usage. 
  • Inference requests in blocks of 100 documents and compute hours. 
  • We can upload to the service up to 2000 documents per hour per tenant (each document can have up to 100 pages). 

Premium: Premium edition service plan that includes document information extraction using Generative AI. 

  • Service plan intended for productive usage. 
  • Inference requests in blocks of 100 documents. 
  • We can upload to the service up to 2000 documents per hour per tenant (each document can have up to 100 pages)

Note: There is Free and trial offering as well. For this blog, we will be using a trial plan.

For Metering and Licensing, refer this 

Supported Document types and formats 

  • Invoice 
  • Business Card (only via API) 
  • paymentAdvice 
  • purchaseOrder 

Document Information Extraction supports the following document file formats as input: 

  • Document files in PDF format 
  • Single-page document files in JPEG, PNG, and TIFF format 
  • Image files that include scene text in JPEG, PNG, and TIFF format 
  • E-invoice document files in PDF and XML hybrid format, and in Factur-X and ZUGFeRD standards (all versions) 
  • payment Advice document files in Excel format 

For information related to supported languages and regions, refer this.

What are the DOX benefits? 

With Document Information Extraction we can: 

  • Process documents more efficiently with fewer errors and difficulties. 
  • Increase quality and compliance mechanisms. 
  • Reduce the time required to process a document. 
  • Allow your organization's members to focus on more relevant tasks in their expertise
  • Leverage Generative AI capabilities with Premium edition to automate document processing

My use case scenario 

The requirement is straightforward, we want to extract information from SAP standard documents (Invoice, Purchase, Sales Orders etc.) or even custom documents like taxi expense receipts for further processing. 

Using the Document Information Extraction UI is much easier than API based usage, hence the objective of this blog is to provide an overview of DOX APIs and how to leverage them in custom applications build to enable the OCR capabilities.

DOX architecture(use case)

 

If you wish to explore Document Information Extraction UI then follow this tutorial

Prerequisites 

1) SAP Document Information Extraction (Trial account): Refer this tutorial for spinning off free DOX trial account 

2) SAP BTP trial account: Refer this tutorial for setting up a free BTP trial account for UI5 application development

In order to explore and consume DOX APIs, first, an service key needs to be created, to authenticate and authorize the incoming requests. Please refer this tutorial to generate a service key from instance 

Let's walkthrough and understand the DOX API's first via an HTTP tool(Postman) and then we jump into BTP UI5 application development part. Using the DOX APIs, we will be able to extract documents information in just 3 steps.

 

1. Get OAuth access token

Below are the mandatory parameters which needs to be entered to generate an access token successfully.

 

OperationGET
URLhttps://{{uaa url from the DOX service key}}/oauth/token?grant_type=client_credentials
Query Params 
grant_type=client_credentials
AuthorizationUsername: clientid from the DOX service key
Password: clientsecret from the DOX service key

Access token call

Note: Access token validity is for 12hours

 

2. Upload/Post document call

Let's take a simple Invoice document pdf with few reference fields we need to extract.

Sample Invoice Document

 

HeaderFields: purchaseOrderNumber,netAmount,senderAddress,currencyCode

LineItemFields: description,netAmount,quantity,unitPrice,materialNumber,unitOfMeasure

 

OperationPOST
URLhttps://{{Backend endpoint url from the DOX service key}}/document-information-extraction/v1/document/jobs
Form-Datafile= {{Upload document file with supported extension}}
Form-Data

options=

{
"extraction": {
"headerFields": [
"purchaseOrderNumber",
"netAmount",
"senderAddress",
"currencyCode"
],
"lineItemFields": [
"description",
"netAmount",
"quantity",
"unitPrice",
"materialNumber",
"unitOfMeasure"
]
},
"clientId": "default"
}
AuthorizationBearer {{Access Token generated from the first call}}

Upload document call

Under options parameter, we need to key in a JSON object which contains reference header and line item fields we need to extract from the document, enrichment parameters (if any), clientId (The ID of the client, this is a mandatory field. For trial account it is default but we can create our own client using Client API)

Note: Detailed optional parameters are well explained in the API reference guide

Upon successful operation of upload document call, the status of the job will be in PENDING status which means the document is uploaded successfully and currently being processed, we can store the request Id which will be further used to get the DOX extraction results.

 

3. Get Extraction results

Extraction results can be retrieved by calling the jobs API with the request id retrieved after the upload call. Once the document processing is successful the status for extracted document will be in "DONE" status.

 

OperationGET
URL

https://{{Backend endpoint url from the DOX service key}}/document-information-extraction/v1/document/jobs/{{document id from the POST call response}}

AuthorizationBearer {{Access Token generated from the first call}}

 

Extract document results call

 Header section extracted fields

 

In the extract results response, header and line items fields will be separated and there is also an extraction confidence level against each field. (This can be used to determine the accuracy of extraction and if needed a manual check step can also be performed in case of automation scenarios)

Now, we have explored and understand the DOX APIs functionality via Postman, it's time to infuse the DOX APIs in BTP UI5 application. 

Infuse DOX APIs in SAP BTP UI5 Application

For the sharing purpose, we have build one lightweight UI5 application via Business Application Studio and deployed it on SAP BTP. It takes the input from user to upload the document(Invoice in our case) as an attachment and once the document is uploaded, backend code will infuse the DOX APIs via BTP Destinations to perform the OCR operation.

BTP Destinations: used to store and retrieve any technical information which is required to connect to a remote service(DOX APIs in our case) from the application deployed in BTP. 1st destination is to get the access token and other destination to call DOX APIs.

Destination for token

Destination for DOX APIs

Once the destinations are maintained, we can proceed with the Business Application Studio to start building the UI5 application. Below is the code snippet which takes the upload file as input, push it to DOX APIs for the document upload call and then finally extracts the results out of the completed document job.

 

 

onUpload: function () {
                var that = this;

                var oFileUpload = this.getView().byId("fileUploaderScanner");
                var oUploadedFile = oFileUpload.oFileUpload.files[0];
                const blob = new Blob([oUploadedFile], { type: oUploadedFile.type });

                var oOptions = {
                    extraction: {
                        headerFields: ["purchaseOrderNumber", "netAmount", "senderAddress", "currencyCode"],
                        lineItemFields: [
                            "description",
                            "netAmount",
                            "quantity",
                            "unitPrice",
                            "materialNumber",
                            "unitOfMeasure"
                        ]
                    },
                    clientId: "default"
                };

                var oFormData = new FormData();
                oFormData.append("options", JSON.stringify(oOptions));
                oFormData.append("file", blob, oUploadedFile.name);

                var oScanModel = new JSONModel();
                oScanModel.loadData("oauth/token", "grant_type=client_credentials", {
                    "Content-Type": "application/json"
                });
                oScanModel.attachRequestCompleted(
                    function (oData) {
                        var sAccessToken = oData.getSource().getProperty("/access_token");

                        var oHeaders = {
                            Accept: "application/json",
                            "X-Requested-With": "XMLHttpRequest",
                            Authorization: "Bearer " + sAccessToken
                        };

                        const requestOptions = {
                            method: "POST",
                            headers: oHeaders,
                            body: oFormData
                        };

                        fetch("document-information-extraction/v1/document/jobs", requestOptions)
                            .then((response) => response.text())
                            .then((result) => {
                                var oResult = JSON.parse(result);
                                var sJobId = oResult.id;

                                    fetch("document-information-extraction/v1/document/jobs/" + sJobId, {
                                        headers: {
                                            Authorization: "Bearer " + sAccessToken
                                        }
                                    })
                                        .then((response) => response.json())
                                        .then((result) => {
                                            var oNewClaimModel = new JSONModel({
                                                PONumber: result.extraction.headerFields.find(
                                                    (x) => x.name === "purchaseOrderNumber"
                                                ).value,
                                                Vendor: result.extraction.headerFields.find(
                                                    (x) => x.name === "senderAddress"
                                                ).value,
                                                Amount: result.extraction.headerFields.find(
                                                    (x) => x.name === "netAmount"
                                                ).rawValue,
                                                Receipt: result.fileName
                                            });
                                            that.getView().setModel(oNewClaimModel);
                                        })
                                        .catch((error) => console.error(error));
                            })
                            .catch((error) => console.error(error));
                    }.bind(this)
                );
            },

 

 

Final outcome: Once the UI5 application is deployed, below is the user input screen which takes the document as input and once it is submitted, the backend code will call the DOX APIs in below sequence.

1) Access token will be fetched

2) Submitted document will be uploaded in the /documents DOX API,

3) Once the document is processed by DOX(probably within few seconds) then extractions results can be fetched via Job Id (as explained in Postman testing section)

4) After getting the extraction results, relevant fields can be mapped to appear in the application UI

 

Invoice Submission UIUpload document UI

Extraction results UI

 

With this blog, we have explored the DOX capability(powered by Generative AI) and how to infuse the DOX APIs for any custom scenarios where OCR is required.

 

Happy Extracting!! 🤖

 

Spoiler
In case, you want to infuse DOX within any Automation or Workflow scenario then SAP Build Process Automation service also offers Document Information Extraction SDK. Refer this page to explore more about DOX in SAP Build Process Automation.

Important links to consider:

1) API reference with common error codes and status: Link

2) Limitations and technical constraints: Link

3) SAP help for DOX: Link

Disclaimer: Above post is purely based on the personal learning and to showcase the basics of how we can leverage DOX APIs for the OCR requirements. Feel free to explore more and improvise your developments as per your particular use cases. Happy to hear comments and feedback.

entry.title: Re: ADSO fields getting empty post data activation
entry.content:
I would suggest to first really check the KBA that was provided by @MKreitlein. Also ensure, that before and after image pairs of the same key are processed within the same package. Otherwise it can happen that the before image is processed on it's own, which leads to the initialization of data fields.

Also check:

  • https://help.sap.com/docs/SUPPORT_CONTENT/bwdabc/4060289327.html?locale=en-US

entry.title: Goods Receipt reversal is not allowed after "Putaway Confirmation". What's next ?
entry.content:
The idea of this blog is to explain one of situations where warehouse clerk has received the stocks by mistake in the system and a goods receipt reversal process is no longer possible due to a system restriction i.e. " Standard GRR is not allowed after putaway confirmation" as described in OSS note - 2551912 - Goods Receipt Reverse not possible by EWM inbound delivery - SAP for Me.

As per standard, if the putaway status is "Completed" for the stock, that means it is putawayed to final bin and it has no delivery reference anymore. Therefore, GRR is not possible further. 

Solution - 

You must create a return delivery in this case. As per system, it interprets that goods have physically arrived at the warehouse however, they need to be returned now. 

In ECC or S/4HANA system, go to Tcode->VL60(Inbound Delivery Processing)->Select Quantity Differences->Enter return quantities to create a "Return Delivery". Delivery then passed over to EWM. you can then pick the corresponding stock for outbound delivery order and perform PGI. 

Pre-requisites to enable a return delivery for quantities in ECC - 

1. A quantity difference profile is created and assigned for the confirmation control key used in the PO. Setting is available in MM->Purchasing->Confirmations->Set Up confirmation Control.

 

2. "via Delivery" indicator must be set for the quantity difference profile. Logistics execution->Services Parts Management->Extended Inbound delivery processing->UI profiles-> Profile:Quantity Differences Shipping Notif./inb. Delivery. 

3. "Delivery Split by warehouse Number" indicator is set for the delivery type used in confirmation. 

I hope the information that i have shared will be helpful. 

Swadhinata Mudali 

Business Process Consultant

Digital Supply Chain Management -Cloud ERP Team

entry.title: Digital Twin Administration, Your Gateway to Advanced Data Ecosystems
entry.content:
As industries increasingly rely on comprehensive data integration to streamline operations and enhance decision-making, the necessity for standardized frameworks becomes apparent. This blog post explores how standardized Digital Twins, guided by the Asset Administration Shell (AAS) standards, play a pivotal role in constructing robust data ecosystems. SAP’s Digital Twin Administration, aligning with Asset Administration Shell standards, offers a scalable and secure registry to manage your digital twins effectively within data ecosystem environments. We invite you to join us in refining and advancing this technology, helping shape the future of digital twins together.

 

Digital Twin and Asset Administration Shell

Digital Twin is the digital counterpart of a physical entity, ranging from objects and processes to entire organizations or abstract concepts. Asset Administration Shell (AAS) represents an industry standard—essentially a guidebook—that describes the administrative and business aspects of a Digital Twin as defined by the Industrial Digital Twin Association (IDTA).

The Importance of Standardized Digital Twins

  • Enhancing System and Company Interoperability 
    In our highly connected industrial world, seamless communication between systems is essential. Standardized Digital Twins ensure that various systems, often from different companies, can interact without issues. This interoperability is crucial for integrating complex supply chains and manufacturing processes, facilitating smooth data flow across various business operations.
  • Optimizing Operations and Reducing Costs 
    Interoperable Digital Twins provide a comprehensive view of business operations, improving resource management and operational strategies. With a unified data structure, companies can cut down on redundancies, reduce errors, and lower costs related to maintenance and system upgrades.
  • Ensuring Compliance and Future Viability
    Standardized Digital Twins help businesses stay compliant with ongoing and upcoming regulations that demand stringent data management and security. By adhering to established standards, companies can safeguard their technology investments against rapid technological shifts, maintaining relevance and compatibility.

Data Ecosystems

What Are Data Ecosystems?

Data ecosystems are networks where data producers, consumers, and intermediaries collaborate to collect, share, analyze, and use data. These ecosystems, characterized by their collaborative nature, enable better decision-making and efficiency by leveraging shared data resources.

In an industrial setting, data ecosystems are vital for enabling digital transformations, ensuring that data flows smoothly and interoperably among various systems and stakeholders.

Industry-Specific Data Ecosystems

Here are some examples of industry-specific data ecosystems that cater to particular sector needs:

  1. Catena-X for the Automotive Industry: A collaborative environment designed to enhance efficiency and innovation across the automotive value chain by standardizing data exchange.
  2. Factory-X for Manufacturing: Supports smart manufacturing and Industry 4.0, promoting data-driven automation and decision-making to boost productivity and quality.
  3. Aerospace-X for the Aerospace Industry: Streamlines data sharing within the aerospace sector to enhance operational efficiency, safety, and innovation.
  4. Process-X for Process Industries: Optimizes data integration and analytics in industries like chemical and pharmaceutical, ensuring quality and regulatory compliance.
  5. Semiconductor-X for the Semiconductor Industry: Addresses the unique challenges of the semiconductor sector, facilitating efficient data sharing across the supply chain.

The Role of Asset Administration Shell in Data Ecosystems

Asset Administration Shell (AAS) is a standardized framework crucial for data exchange within industrial ecosystems. It provides a structured format for describing and managing information about assets, ensuring seamless integration and interoperability across different systems and stakeholders.

 

Introducing SAP's Digital Twin Administration

SAP's Digital Twin Administration acts as a comprehensive Digital Twin Registry conforming to Asset Administration Shell standards. This service, running on the SAP Business Technology Platform, is crafted meticulously for businesses operating within or looking to join data-rich environments such as Catena-X, Factory-X, Aerospace-X, Process-X, and Semiconductor-X.

Why Opt for SAP’s Digital Twin Administration?

Choosing this solution enhances operational efficiency and connects you with a network of industry leaders and innovators. It offers:

  • Seamless Integration: Works smoothly with other SAP solutions, creating a cohesive operational framework.
  • Enhanced Data Management: By facilitating structured and accessible data ecosystems, our product ensures that you can manage your assets and operational data with unprecedented ease and accuracy.
  • Scalability and Security: Adapts to business growth while maintaining strict security standards essential for managing critical data.

Join Us!

We’re excited to not only offer this innovative product but also to invite you to help us refine and perfect it. Your insights are invaluable as we enhance the Digital Twin Administration. Join us in driving the future of digital twin technology.

Contact us today (email) to see how the Digital Twin Administration can transform your business operations.

At SAP, we’re building more than just software—we’re laying the groundwork for the next wave of industrial innovation. Join us in leveraging digital twins to take your business to new heights.

entry.title: Enhancing Field Service: SAP Business AI Unveils Intelligent Filtering and Equipment Insights
entry.content:
During my last vacation, I stayed at a charming flat on a farm. It was the perfect escape—serene landscapes, fresh air, and the rustic charm of rural life. However, there was one minor hiccup: the heating wasn't working properly, making the stay slightly less comfortable. The property owner sent a technician who took a few days to figure out the problem. Though it was just a bit annoying, I couldn't help but think how much better it would have been if the issue had been fixed before my arrival, sparing me even that small inconvenience.

Now, imagine a different scenario where the technician arrives before I even notice the heating is broken and fixes the issue seamlessly. This is the subtle yet powerful impact of Generative AI (GenAI)—not a dramatic change out of a sci-fi movie, but a small improvement that makes life and work a bit more convenient, like an annoying fly that suddenly stops bothering you.

The GenAI Revolution: Transforming Our World

Generative AI is quietly revolutionizing how we interact with technology, making complex processes simpler and more intuitive. In our everyday lives, we may not always notice these changes, but they significantly enhance our convenience and efficiency. In the realm of field service management, GenAI is making a notable impact, streamlining both dispatching and execution.

Impact on Field Service Dispatching and Execution

Field service dispatching traditionally relies on manual processes and people’s experience, often resulting in delays and inefficiencies. This is especially impactful when experienced personnel retire, and a new generation takes over. The knowledge gap can lead to slower response times and incorrect assessments. With GenAI, dispatching is transformed. AI algorithms analyze vast amounts of data to predict service needs, optimize routes, and allocate resources more efficiently. This ensures technicians are dispatched promptly and equipped with the right tools and information, leading to faster and more effective service delivery.

For execution, GenAI provides real-time insights and support to technicians on-site. This includes intelligent filtering of information, predictive maintenance alerts, and comprehensive equipment insights, ensuring that technicians can diagnose and resolve issues swiftly and accurately. For instance, in my farm stay scenario, the faulty component would have sent a trigger, alerting the technician before I even realized there was a problem, allowing for a seamless and timely fix.

Embracing GenAI: A Technician's Perspective

Technicians, often burdened with administrative tasks like report writing, searching for relevant information, and navigating through multiple software interfaces, can greatly benefit from GenAI. By automating these time-consuming tasks, GenAI allows technicians to focus on what they do best—solving technical problems and providing excellent service.

  • Automated Reporting: GenAI can generate detailed service reports automatically, saving technicians from the tedious task of manual documentation.
  • Efficient Information Retrieval: Equipment Insights quickly provides technicians with the most relevant information, reducing the time spent searching through manuals or databases.
  • Natural Language Interaction: The first step towards a more intuitive interaction with software, intelligent filtering based on natural language processing (NLP) allows technicians to speak to the software to get what they need instead of typing, clicking, and browsing. This makes information retrieval faster and more user-friendly.

With these capabilities, technicians can enhance their productivity, reduce job-related stress, and deliver higher-quality service to customers.

New Challenges for Dispatchers in a Dynamic World

As machines become more complex and specialized, the role of dispatchers evolves. Relying solely on decades of experience is no longer sufficient. Dispatchers must now leverage advanced AI tools to match technicians with tasks based on specific skills and the intricacies of modern machinery.

  • Dynamic Skill Matching: AI-driven systems analyze technician skills and job requirements in real-time, ensuring the right technician is assigned to the right job.
  • Continuous Learning: Dispatchers need to stay updated with the latest AI tools and technologies to make informed decisions in a rapidly changing environment.
  • Enhanced Coordination: With AI handling many logistical aspects, dispatchers can focus on strategic planning and coordination, improving overall service efficiency.

Introducing Intelligent Filtering and Equipment Insights

 

Intelligent Filtering

Intelligent Filtering in SAP Field Service Management uses natural language processing to translate sentences into queries that can be run on the database to retrieve relevant jobs for dispatching. For example, a dispatcher might write, "Give me all critical jobs in a 50 km radius around Berlin that require a certain skill." This query retrieves the necessary jobs, allowing the dispatcher to assign the most urgent tasks to a technician in Berlin with the specific skills needed.

This feature enhances search accuracy, offers predictive suggestions, and improves resource allocation, ultimately saving time and increasing efficiency for field service managers and technicians.

Equipment Insights

Equipment Insights is an AI-driven feature that generates a summary based on previous activities performed on a piece of equipment. The summary includes information about used spare parts, the duration of past activities, performed checklists, and their results. This comprehensive overview allows technicians to resolve issues more quickly and accurately. Additionally, it helps dispatchers make better decisions about assigning new activities by providing a clear history of the equipment's maintenance and issues.

Benefits include:

  • Anomaly Detection: Identifies unusual patterns or behaviors in equipment performance, enabling proactive measures to prevent issues.
  • Comprehensive Equipment History: Allows technicians to view detailed histories and patterns, aiding in faster diagnostics and repairs.
  • Predictive Maintenance: Reduces downtime by predicting potential failures before they occur.

SAP Business AI: Relevant, Reliable, Responsible

Generative AI capabilities in SAP Field Service Management are built on SAP Business AI. With SAP Business AI, we build the system of intelligence with three core principles: Relevant, Reliable, Responsible.

These principles ensure that our solutions deliver real business results:

  • Relevant: Everything is embedded into our leading enterprise applications and business technology solutions. Customers can count on their SAP systems to be future-proofed with the most relevant AI experiences in the context of their business.
  • Reliable: Based on unique business data and business process context, we deliver the most reliable business AI. Our AI applications are only as good as the data, and SAP has the most important data and business context intact.
  • Responsible: We deliver AI with the highest levels of concern for security, privacy, compliance, and ethics. SAP builds and runs powerful, trustworthy solutions, including AI that touches the most vital data and systems of our customers.

Conclusion

SAP Business AI is revolutionizing Field Service Management with its Intelligent Filtering and Equipment Insights features. By leveraging GenAI, field service operations become more efficient, technicians are empowered, and customer satisfaction is elevated. In this fast-changing world, embracing advanced AI tools is essential for staying ahead, ensuring that field service management is not only modern but also strategically advanced.

With AI-driven capabilities in SAP Field Service Management, the future of field service management is intelligent, efficient, and customer-centric, transforming everyday frustrations into seamless, effective service experiences.

To see and hear more about the Benefits and Demo of the new AI Services for SAP Field Service Management sign up or watch recording: Webcast

To read more about the latest features for SAP Field Service Management go to: Blog

To learn more about the SAP Field Service Management solution go to: Product Page

To learn more about the SAP's Business AI strategy go to: White Paper

To learn more about the SAP’s AI ethics policy go to: SAP Global AI Ethics Policy

To read more about State of Generative AI in the Enterprise go to: Deloitte’s State of Generative AI in the Enterprise

entry.title: Technical User Propagation from JCo towards On-Premises
entry.content:
This blog lays out how to use a technical user instead of basic authentication from JCo based on the SAP Java Buildpack in CF towards on-premises.

 

Background

JCo retrieves an access token representing the technical user which is then be sent to the Connectivity service. This is similar to principal propagation, but in this case, a technical user is propagated instead of a business user. The retrieval of the access token performs the OAuth 2.0 client credentials flow, according to the token service configurations in the destination. Currently for JCo the token service generation supports basic authentication only. The token service is called from the Internet, not from the Cloud Connector.

 

Configuration

Generally speaking, the setup as described in the documentation stays the same, only the destination configuration in the Destination Service needs to be adjusted.

In the UI select the authentication type TechnicalUserPropagation. You now need to enter three values for:

  • jco.client.tech_user_id - the technical user name (client ID) which is forwarded towards on-premises and used for token retrieval
  • jco.client.tech_user_secret - the secret for jco.client.tech_user_id used for token retrieval
  • jco.client.tech_user_service_url - the URL of the token service, against which the token exchange is performed

Example

We are going to use the token of the XSUAA service instance here. We specified for the instance in the configuration JSON the xsappname as jco-technicalProp.

After the application binding we can retrieve the relevant parameters from the CF environment variables VCAP_SERVICES:

 

"clientid": "sb-jco-technicalProp!t77058"
"clientsecret": "TMsePptYQLSRf6qUWWt+l1D0rUQ="
"url": "https://cf.authentication.hana.ondemand.com"

 

Entering it in the Destination Service:

The token will now be forwarded to the Cloud Connector. Assuming all necessary basic steps for principal propagation are configured, we can configure a pattern to extract its name for the short-lived certificate:

The ABAP backend needs to maintain a user mapping for this technical user, in this case mapping it to the ABAP user SKYWALKER:

That's it!

entry.title: Powering Logistics with SAP Business Technology Platform
entry.content:
Logistics and the last mile

Organisations often face the challenge of having to meet a highly variable demand with a relatively fixed operational infrastructure. Whilst smart manufacturing drives up efficiencies, reduces waste, etc., a critical component of the “lead-to-cash” cycle is product availability for customers. Logistics is the “last mile” of the supply chain: it is the last stop before the product reaches the customer. This is where organisations win or lose the battle for the status of preferred supplier of choice.

Despite its significance, logistics continues to deal with many pressures and deliverables. Efforts must be accelerated to address overstocked warehouses, inefficient distribution networks and multiple product handling, which adversely impact service levels and carbon footprint.

But these challenges also offer opportunities.  Reduced picking, loading and transport movements all combine to lower inventory levels and storage footprints (and less land converted into warehousing sites). This is in addition to contributing to the sustainability goals of the organisation through reduced energy usage in storage and distribution centres, and through improved fleet utilisation.

 

Digital Supply Chains

With SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP), organisations can continuously innovate and evolve with platform solutions. Organisations can take advantage of over 1,000 prebuilt data models, integrations, workflows, app templates and AI business services. SAP BTP provides customers with the “best of both worlds”: able to use SAP BTP services in conjunction with services from their hyperscaler of choice. Did you know that SAP BTP works very well with ECC and is suitable for both private and public cloud editions of SAP S/4HANA?

SAP BTP is not just another platform. It provides a means to integrate, extend, and build data to value at scale across supply chain operations while adding value to organisations current investments, expertise, and supplier network.

Why does this matter? Well, a critical review of current supply chain tools sometimes results in the conclusion that invested solutions need to be replaced. SAP BTP allows organisations to positively review their current investments and build upon these to further their transformation journey without the need to “rip or replace” current solutions. Every organisation has their own view of leading practices and where they fit. SAP BTP augments and complements supply chain solutions to meet the specific needs of the customer.

Logistics practitioners will be more than familiar with the need for manual processing of orders and delivery notes, and all the physical activities which are part of in-bound logistics, inventory management and stock reconciliation. But what if we could perform automated checks of the information in high volumes of delivery notes, automatically choose the most optimised storage configuration, determine stock levels at a precise SKU level in real-time, adjust locations of goods according to the market situation, etc. What if we could have insights about future utilization of the internal logistics network, and also data-driven insights for the set-up of contracts with logistics carriers.

 Value Proposition

SAP BTP reimagines supply chains and powers logistics. It enables organisations to personalise the extension of their current capabilities of supply chain processes or go beyond merely “filling gaps” by placing leading practices within their grasp. Used in this way, SAP BTP connects end-to-end processes and build customisations in SAP Extended Warehouse Management (EWM) and SAP Transportation Management (TM).

Examples of Use Cases

Below are two use cases which serve as examples of how SAP BTP is deployed to enhance and extend the capabilities of SAP EWM and SAP TM to drive specific outcomes.

The first use case shows what can be achieved when SAP TM is combined with SAP BTP. Here, logistics optimisation improves visibility across logistics operations and network. Decision makers often establish contracts with trucking companies over the next six months with almost no data-driven insights. The solution shows decision makers which segments will be over- or undersized and when. Additionally, they can adjust parameters to immediately visualize the impact on truck route utilization on certain days. This provides insights on the future utilization of the internal logistics network to save costs and improve SLA by adapting capacity to match demand.

 

Challenges overcome:

  • High logistics costs across service providers and contracts.
  • Huge manual effort to meet new orders and customer deliveries with short lead times.
  • Lack of full visibility of the total distribution network.
  • Inefficient fleet utilisation which leads to high carbon footprint.

Value-drive benefits include:

  • Improved delivery and reduced logistics costs by streamlining operations with overall network view.
  • Reduction in fleet with optimal planning, re-planning for new orders and modifications to existing ones.
  • Visibility across logistics operations and faster response to issues.
  • Insights on future utilization of logistics network to save costs and improve SLAs.

 

The second use case shows how SAP BTP and SAP EWM come together provide intelligent storage locations. This helps the customer to adjust locations of goods within the warehouse in real-time. As an example, it is beneficial to place goods with high sales in locations closer to the exit of the automatic sorting and picking system, which shorts transmission distance and picking time. Intelligent storage location also provides guidance for stock and supplier replenishment operations based on real-time sales data.

Challenges overcome:

  • Inventory fluctuations and a lack of inventory transparency.
  • Inability to deliver on time leading to lost sales as well as overstocked inventory and increasing costs.
  • Unable to determine stock levels at a precise SKU level in real-time to adjust locations of goods according to the market situation.

Value-driven benefits include:

  • Increase in sales, visibility, and transparency.
  • Analyze real-time information about stock levels on SKU level provided by IoT sensors in storage locations.
  • Data can be analyzed and displayed in real-time, enabling monitoring of global inventory levels.

 

 

Customer Proof Point

We can bring these use cases to life with a customer example. A North American customer organisation in the telecom industry was facing operational issues including:

  • No real-time inventory leading to low fulfillment rates.
  • Poor customer service levels.
  • Inability to allocate orders leading to manual processing and paper-based prioritisation of picking.
  • High cost-to-serve through an inability to cross charge shipping charges to their customers for expedited shipments. 

With SAP BTP, the customer organisation was able to overcome these issues and realise significant benefits:

  • 20% reduction in days in inventory.
  • 30% reduction in inventory carrying costs.
  • 40% reduction in total logistics costs.

 

Summary

SAP BTP is a technology platform which brings together into one place: analytics, integration, application development and automation, and AI:

  • Enables organisations to future proof their end-to-end supply chain processes without the need to “rip or replace” current solutions.
  • Augments and complements supply chain solutions to meet the specific needs of customers.
  • Connects end-to-end processes and build customizations in warehouse and transportation solutions to address competing pressures and deliverables.
  • Many use cases demonstrate the proven capabilities of SAP BTP across warehousing and delivery operations.

 

Further information

  • You can learn more about SAP BTP on the SAP Community
  • For an overview of how SAP BTP provides solutions to support supply chain transformation, please read our SAP BTP for Supply Chain Point of View Paper.
  • Integrated into the SAP BTP page, a BTP for DSC section now exists: Reimagine Your Supply Chain

entry.title: AI in Test Automation
entry.content:
The use of artificial intelligence (AI) in test automation is the latest trend in quality assurance. Testing, in general, and test automation, in particular, seems to have caught the “everything's better with AI” bug.

Since AI, machine learning, and neural networks are the hottest thing right now, it is perhaps inevitable that AI would find its way into test automation somehow.

A Background in Test Automation

I recall a time several years ago, back when test automation was still new, when one of my quality assurance teams was working on a project for a large customer. It was a mobile application with millions of users and monthly release cycles. The QA team was usually relaxed during the first two weeks of the cycle and working frantically thereafter till release—one of the peculiar side effects of agile software development that you don’t read about in the headlines!

Finally, one of the QA leads, fed up with twiddling his thumbs during the lean two weeks, started working on a framework for test automation. He wrote up some test scripts in Ruby with Selenium / Appium and Jenkins for a rudimentary pipeline, including some pretty reports with Red / Yellow / Green indicators for test failure/success.

He successfully did this for a couple of release cycles. That’s when we presented it to our Quality Chief, who pitched it to our customers and got them excited enough to pay for automated testing as a sub-project. We were on cloud nine, on the bleeding edge of things!

Bleeding edge took on a new meaning, however, in a few months when we discovered that fundamental truth about test automation that any QA automation engineer worth their salt wrestles with today

Test automation has not fundamentally changed since then because it still requires continuous monitoring and maintenance. Any possibility for dramatic improvement in the approach and implementation has only appeared with the recent advancement in the capabilities of AI.

Will AI actually be able to help automatically generate and update test cases? Find bugs? Improve code coverage?

The answer to that question is far from clear right now because we’re at the peak of the hype cycle for AI. A specific sub-field, deep learning, has caused a lot of this excitement.

How Does Machine Learning Produce Automated Tests?

  1. Training: In the training phase, the machine learning model needs to be trained on a specific organizational dataset, including the codebase, application interface, logs, test cases, and even specification documents. Not having a sufficiently large training dataset can reduce the efficacy of the algorithm.

    Some tools have pre-trained models that are updated through continuous learning for specific applications like UI testing so that generalized learning can be used in a specific organization.

  2. Output / Result generation: Depending on the use case, the model generates test cases, checks existing test cases for code coverage, completeness, accuracy, and even performs tests. In all cases, a tester needs to check the output generated to get validation and ensure that it is usable. If we use the analogy of self-driving cars, the results are more like driving assistance than an actual driverless car.
  3. Continuous improvement: As an organization keeps using the tool regularly, the training data keeps increasing, thereby potentially increasing the accuracy and effectiveness of existing trained networks. In short, the AI system keeps learning and improving.

 

Applications Of AI In Test Automation

Let's take a closer look at some applications of AI in test automation including unit testing , user interface testing, API testing , and maintaining an automation test suite.

Creating And Updating Unit Tests

Unit testing , often used as part of continuous testing, continuous integration / continuous delivery (CI / CD) in DevOps , can be a real pain in the… asteroid belt.

Typically, developers spend significant amounts of time authoring and maintaining unit tests, which is nowhere near as much fun as writing application code. In this instance, AI-based products for automated unit test creation can be useful, especially for those organizations that plan to introduce unit tests late in the product life cycle.

Benefits: 

  • AI-based automated unit tests are a significant step ahead of template-based automated unit test generation using static or dynamic analysis. The tests so generated are actual code, not just stubs.
  • AI-based unit tests can be generated very quickly, which is useful for a large existing codebase.
  • Developers just need to modify tests and can set up the unit regression suite relatively quickly.

Limitations: 

  • AI-generated unit tests simply mirror the code on which they are built. They cannot as yet guess the intended functionality of the code. If the code does not behave as intended, the unit test generated for that code will reflect that unintended behavior.

    This is a significant minus because the whole point of unit tests is to enforce and verify an implicit or explicit contract.
  • Unit tests generated using machine learning can break existing working unit tests, and it is up to a developer to ensure that this does not happen.
  • Developers need to write tests for complex business logic themselves.

Automated User Interface Testing 

This is an area where AI is beginning to shine. In AI-based UI testing, test automation tools parse the DOM and related code to ascertain object properties. They also use image recognition techniques to navigate through the application and verify UI objects and elements visually to create UI tests.

Additionally, AI test systems use exploratory testing to find bugs or variations in the application UI and generate screenshots for later verification by a QA engineer. Similarly, the visual aspects of the System Under Test (SUT) such as layout, size, and color can be verified.

Benefits:

  • Automated UI testing can lead to increased code coverage.
  • Minor deviations in the UI do not cause the test suite to fail. Product AI models can handle these.

Limitations:

  • For any modern application, the number of platforms, app versions, and browser versions is large. It is not clear how well AI-based UI automation performs under these conditions. However, cloud testing tools can execute tests in parallel, so this is going to be an interesting space to watch!

Using AI To Assist In API Testing

Even without AI, automating API testing is a non-trivial task since it involves understanding the API and then setting up tests for a multitude of scenarios to ensure depth and breadth of coverage.

Current API test automation tools , like Tricentis and SoapUI, record API activities and traffic to analyze and create tests. However, modifying and updating tests require testers to delve into the minutiae of REST calls and parameters, and then update the API test suite.

AI-based API automation testing  tools attempt to mitigate this problem by examining traffic and identifying patterns and connections between API calls, effectively grouping them by scenario. Tools also use existing tests to learn about relationships between APIs, use these to understand changes in APIs, and update existing tests or create new scenario-based tests. 

Benefits:

  • For novice testers or those without programming experience, this could be really helpful to get them to “hit the ground running”.

    Once again, change management would be significantly easier given that at least some of the API changes can be handled by an AI automation tool.

Limitations:

  • In general, API testing is difficult to set up, and not many tools offer machine learning based capabilities in this space. The ones that do seem to have pretty rudimentary capabilities.

Automation Test Maintenance

AI-based tools can evaluate changes to the code and fix several existing tests that don't align with those changes, especially if those code changes are not too complex. Updates to UI elements, field names, and the like need not break the test suite anymore.

Some AI tools monitor running tests and try out modified variants for failed tests by choosing UI elements based on the best fit. They can also verify test coverage and supplement the gaps if needed. 

 

AI-Based Test Data Generation

Test data generation is another promising area for AI models. Machine learning can easily generate data sets, like personal profile photographs and information like age and weight, based on trained machine learning models using existing production datasets to learn. 

In this way, the test data generated is very similar to production data which is ideal for use in software testing . The machine learning model that generates data is called a Generative Adversarial Network  (GAN).

 

Conclusion

Artificial intelligence has significantly impacted testing tools and methods, and test automation in particular. An overview of the current tools promising AI shows that, while many new features are being added, several of those features are still on their way to maturity.

entry.title: Re: Billing on Future Date
entry.content:
Hello @sambtr ,

If you are looking to change the billing date when creating a billing document you can do so in the app Create Billing Documents. Please select the button Billing Settings first and make sure the selection set Billing Date and Type before billing is turned on. You can then select the items from the list that should be included in the billing document and click the button Create Billing Documents. A popup shows that allows to choose the billing due date.

Kind regards,

Tobias

entry.title: Support for multiple user roles in Integration flows sender Adapters (HTTP, SOAP, REST, ODATA etc)
entry.content:
Hello Everyone,

I have a scenario where I have two packages PackageA and PackageB both packages contain Synchronous iflows with HTTP Sender adapter,

PackageA iflows are accessible only via custom role PackageAMessaging.send 

and 

PackageB iflows are accessible only via custom role PackageBMessaging.send 

The reason I have this package based UserRole setup is that these packages are called by individual systems and house all the iflows being used only by one particular sender (so far we didnt have any overlaps between packages)

Now PackageA consumer needs to access another iFlow that exists in PackageB that happens to be an ODATA API exposed via the http sender adapter. Http Sender adapter doesnt have a provision for multiple Userroles and supports only one user role at a time. I want to avoid duplicating this iflow on another http sender endpoint and I want to reuse the existing iflow. How can I do that?

entry.title: Error: 'Payroll area not found in T549A. IT0001 Error' in ECPAO_EE_ORG_REPL_QUERY program
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

I have hired new employees in SuccessFactors EC with Payroll area 'A1' maintained compensation information portlet. All the Org objects are replicated to ECC Successfully using SFIOM_QRY_ORG_OBJ - Org Object Replication Query Program.

When i replicate employee master data for new hires using the program ECPAO_EE_ORG_REPL_QUERY, i am getting error 'Payroll area is not found in T549A. Info type 0001 Error' in SLG1 log. However, noticed there is no issue T549A configuration, and i am able to hire employee directly in ECC with the same payroll area 'A1'.

Note, I have no issues in replicating existing employee belongs to the same payroll area, and the issue is for the New Hires Only. Please guide what i have been missing out. Your help is much appreciated. Thanks

Regards,

JP
